title: FERPA: the privacy law every US SIS must obey
summary: Before you store a single grade, you must know FERPA — the US federal law governing student education records. Who has rights, what counts as 'directory information', and when you may disclose without consent. (US-specific; other regions have their own laws like GDPR.)

activities.title: What FERPA is
activities.markdown:
**FERPA** (Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act) is a **US federal law** that gives parents — and **'eligible students'** (age 18+ or enrolled in a postsecondary institution) — the right to:

- **inspect** their education records,
- seek to **amend** them, and
- have some control over the **disclosure** of personally identifiable information (PII) from those records.

The statute is **20 U.S.C. § 1232g**; the regulations are **34 CFR Part 99**. It applies to schools that receive funds from the U.S. Department of Education. ⚠️ FERPA is **US-specific** — the EU's GDPR and other national laws govern elsewhere.

sources.title: What is FERPA? — U.S. Dept of Education (Student Privacy)

activities.question: Under FERPA, when do the parent's rights transfer to the student (becoming an 'eligible student')?
options: When the student turns 18 OR enrolls in a postsecondary institution
Never — parents always hold the rights
activities.explanation: FERPA rights transfer to the 'eligible student' when they reach age 18 or enroll in a postsecondary institution at any age. For universities, that means the student — not the parent — generally holds FERPA rights.

sources.title: What is FERPA? — U.S. Dept of Education

activities.title: Directory information & the 'school official' exception
activities.markdown:
Two ideas you'll encode into an SIS's access rules:

- **Directory information** (34 CFR § 99.3): info that wouldn't generally be considered harmful if disclosed — e.g., **name, address, dates of attendance**. A school may disclose it **without consent** *if* it gives public notice and lets students **opt out**. It **excludes** things like SSN.
- **The 'school official with a legitimate educational interest' exception** (§ 99.31): prior consent is **not** required to disclose to school officials who need the records to do their job — and this can include **contractors** performing outsourced institutional functions (relevant when ManageArk is the vendor!).

activities.question: Which disclosure is generally allowed WITHOUT prior written consent under FERPA?
options: To a school official with a legitimate educational interest
Publicly posting grades labeled by SSN
Handing records to anyone who asks
activities.explanation: § 99.31 permits disclosure without consent to school officials (which can include contractors performing institutional services) who have a legitimate educational interest. The others are classic FERPA violations.

activities.question: Which is typically eligible to be 'directory information' (disclosable without consent unless the student opts out)?
options: A student's name and dates of attendance
activities.explanation: Directory information is limited to items not generally considered harmful, like name and dates of attendance. SSN, grades, and disciplinary records are NOT directory information.

activities.title: FERPA disclosure decision
activities.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A{Education record?} -->|no| Z[FERPA does not restrict]
  A -->|yes| B{Directory info and not opted out?}
  B -->|yes| Y[May disclose]
  B -->|no| C{A 99.31 exception applies?}
  C -->|yes: school official, etc.| Y
  C -->|no| D[Get written consent]
  D --> E[Disclose and log it]

steps.title: Is it an education record?
steps.caption: Records directly related to a student and maintained by the institution.

steps.title: Directory information?
steps.caption: If it's directory info and the student hasn't opted out, you may disclose.

steps.title: Does a §99.31 exception apply?
steps.caption: E.g., a school official with a legitimate educational interest.

steps.title: Otherwise get written consent
steps.caption: No exception → you need the eligible student's written consent.

activities.caption: The core FERPA question is always: do I need consent, or does an exception apply?

activities.front: What is an **'eligible student'** under FERPA?
activities.back: A student who has reached **age 18** OR who is **enrolled in a postsecondary institution** at any age. FERPA rights transfer from the parent to that student.

items: **FERPA** = US law (20 U.S.C. § 1232g / 34 CFR Part 99) protecting education records.
At a **university**, the **student** holds the rights (eligible student).
**Directory information** (name, dates of attendance) is disclosable without consent if the student hasn't opted out; **SSN/grades are not**.
Disclosure to a **school official / contractor with legitimate educational interest** needs no consent — design ManageArk's access model around that exception.

glossary.definition: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act — US federal law (20 U.S.C. § 1232g; 34 CFR Part 99) giving students/parents rights over education records and limiting disclosure of PII.
A record directly related to a student and maintained by an educational institution or party acting for it.
A student who is 18+ or enrolled in a postsecondary institution; FERPA rights transfer from parent to this student.
FERPA category of non-harmful info (e.g., name, dates of attendance) that may be disclosed without consent unless the student opts out; excludes SSN.

reflection: ManageArk will often be the 'school official' contractor under FERPA. How would you encode 'legitimate educational interest' as OpenFGA relationships so access is need-to-know rather than role-blanket?
